comment.body:
speed and vanna have usually the same net exposures, but not always. for vanna you take multiple expos, where vol responses differ.
vanna is also a bridge between implied and realized volatility. 
but it is more like a mirror of the reflexive effects:
like for example:

traders net sell upside calls and buy downside puts = long skew.
dealers will sell the skew, bcs they buy those upside calls and sell those downside puts.
the result is a net positive dealer vanna profile. 

what customers are doing here is bidding up the upside gammas, or in otherwords: selling upside realized volatility. 

now of course, their gamma/RV selling becomes profitable only if the spot is grinding higher.
so if they are kind of "confident" with their bet, the customer flow will be a net seller of IV.
And if IV goes down, delta goes down as well, as the profile is dealer long vanna. The key is to see, in which tenor the selling occurs the most (fixed strike skew analysis on diff tenors).
so this is a supportive flow that creates a constant passive buying flow by the dealers, and will make the spot to grind upward.

comment.body: Just to clarify you can have a negative speed profile - when gamma grows (more positive) below the spot - which essentially means there's a lot of gamma supply below - which makes dealers long the skew & short Vanna. Yes, if there is selling dealers will act as counter-balancing force absorbing the selling flow, resulting in vol compression. But if overall selling exceeds dealer absorption, and implied vol perks up, short vanna will cause dealer to sell deltas - Correct ? Can you please elaborate how doe a positive speed profile (below) ( gamma declines below spot) , and positive speed above ( gamma grows above spot) will effect flow. Or is relative difference between below & upside gamma will determine it
comment.raw_body: Just to clarify you can have a negative speed profile - when gamma grows (more positive) below the spot - which essentially means there's a lot of gamma supply below - which makes dealers long the skew & short Vanna. Yes, if there is selling dealers will act as counter-balancing force absorbing the selling flow, resulting in vol compression. But if overall selling exceeds dealer absorption, and implied vol perks up, short vanna will cause dealer to sell deltas - Correct ? Can you please elaborate how doe a positive speed profile (below) ( gamma declines below spot) , and positive speed above ( gamma grows above spot) will effect flow. Or is relative difference between below & upside gamma will determine it

comment.body: Appreciate the explanation here. So a dealer risk reversal - which is short the skew - create a positive speed profile ( both below the spot & above the spot)  long the Vanna. And a dealer short risk reversal will be long the skew, ( negative speed profile - both below & above the spot) &  short the Vanna. I also read in your post that change in speed profile also produces the same reversal in delta hedging flow as a change in vanna profile.
comment.raw_body: Appreciate the explanation here. So a dealer risk reversal - which is short the skew - create a positive speed profile ( both below the spot & above the spot)  long the Vanna. And a dealer short risk reversal will be long the skew, ( negative speed profile - both below & above the spot) &  short the Vanna. I also read in your post that change in speed profile also produces the same reversal in delta hedging flow as a change in vanna profile.

comment.body: On the weekly post, assuming these are dealer positions, I can clearly see the market selling downside skew, which creates a negative speed profile on the lower end. On the upside, the large bars suggest dealers are short net - net , which also implies a negative speed profile. Overall, it looks like the speed profile steepens significantly as we move from below spot to above spot. Can you please clarify If I am interpreting it correctly

comment.body:
be careful, bcs slopeness is the ratio of BOTH axis! not just one.
so even tho you see gamma bars growing in a direction, it depends how they are dispersed on the horizontal axis.
the slopness will change.
so you can't tell speed profile using your eyes solely.
you need to produce its derivative function
comment.raw_body:

you need to produce its derivative function
